column2: — There's one answer to everything — war.
And right now there's a war.
— Better tell us, Lilichka, how you split up with Aiven.
— There was a war, — Lilichka smiled.
Lilichka, small, pale and timid, had come to meet Aiven's parents.
— And it does mean something, after all, when an Englishman invites you to meet his parents, — said Miyechka.
Marta didn't know this story.
Aiven's mother smiled at Lilichka.
And hugged her in the doorway.
She'd heard somewhere that there, in those wild parts where Aiven had found this small, strange woman, everyone hugs.
— She'd have done better not to, — Lilichka explained.
Her embraces were the kind you couldn't actually touch.
They froze in the air, those embraces.
As a result Mrs. Crumbles only touched Lilichka's shoulder blades with her fingertips, spreading her arms like wings.
— We're so glad to see you!
— said Mrs. Crumbles.
— We've heard so many good things about you!
— said Mr. Crumbles.
In reality they'd heard nothing about Lilichka, and had only read on Wikipedia about the country she came from.
Even for that one could be grateful.

— It's one of those countries that Russia fell apart into, — Mr. Crumbles had said then, sitting at the computer, and, with the concentration only old people are capable of, turned the mouse wheel, having enlarged the font on the screen, while Mrs. Crumbles, her hair combed, lay in bed.
— We have a surprise for you!
— said Mrs. Crumbles, lifting her fingertips off Lilichka's shoulder blades.
— We'll have a guest, a countryman of yours, so you feel a little more at home.
Lilichka smiled and glanced at Aiven.
Aiven looked at his mother.
— We've invited Alex.
Alex had a broad, literary 'Russian face,' fair eyebrows, and went red when he drank.
This, however, only became known later.
Aiven didn't know Alex, but had heard from his mother that a new historian had appeared in their department.
He studied the Russian White émigré movement and was known for his quiet nature and his pliancy.
Aiven's father loved Alex for his skilled hands — he had fixed the chain on his bicycle.
Alex spoke with a harsh Russian accent and came from somewhere near Vyazma, but with his military father he had traveled all over, as he himself put it, 'the great motherland.'
Lilichka looked at Aiven with prayer in her eyes.
— He's a liberal, — Aiven said.
Aiven knew little about politics.
Back then Lilichka could still afford to be undiscriminating when it came to the political preferences of her lovers.
Aiven said everything would be fine.
It was not fine.
Alex began by telling Lilichka to call him Sasha.
Then he switched to democracy and freedom in his historical homeland.
— When the regime finally falls in Russia, — he began, and Aiven's parents nodded at this.
Everyone wanted regimes to fall.
— Then we'll finally become one people, — Aiven finished.
Lilichka picked at her salad, chasing cherry tomatoes around the plate, and then suddenly said, without raising her head:
— If that's what you want to bring down the regime for, then may the Lord send health and long years to your dictator.
Aiven exchanged glances with his parents.
Wishing dictators health and long years was not the custom in this house.
— Lili, — Aiven said, — let's wait for the chicken, it's Mom's signature dish.
Alex didn't know about the chicken and the risk of not getting to eat it, so he went on with the conversation.
— And you couldn't keep quiet?
— the grandmother asked Lilichka while she was telling the story.
— I told him everything.
By then the times when it was acceptable to keep quiet had already passed.
No one got to wait for the chicken.
Mr. and Mrs. Crumbles sat subdued while the enraged Lilichka shouted at Alex, and he finally began calling her a fascist.
The political literacy of the Crumbles family ended at fascism, which was for them the only evil known to history.
Mrs. Crumbles then drank mint tea before bed, trying to calm down, and asked her son whether he really loved Lilichka so much that he was prepared to risk being in a couple with a fascist.
Aiven didn't know what to say to that, though he tried to repeat the words Lilichka had said about oppression and national identity.
None of those words resonated in his soul, so he sounded unconvincing.
— And what, you split up just over an argument?
— asked Marta.
She stood by the window, and half of her pale face, with the swollen lips that very pregnant women have, was lit by the light from the window.
— I poured orange juice over Sasha, — replied Lilichka, — but Aiven didn't understand me.
To him that chicken of his mother's was more important than anything.
— God, how embarrassing, — said the grandmother.
— And did you hit him or the carpet too?
— Him, — Lilichka noted proudly.
Of all the women in the family, only Lilichka was capable of pouring juice over an opponent.
All the rest were calm, polite, and then for years, before falling asleep, conducted imaginary dialogues with the people over whom they hadn't managed to pour juice as a final argument.
And now they all pretended to be embarrassed, but in their hearts they relished it.
— So he changed his mind about uniting with you?
— asked Miyechka, laughing.
— Decided it was safer not to bring down the regime for now, — said Marta.
— But Aiven also changed his mind about uniting, — said Lilichka.
And thank God!
No one will be telling him anymore that his frankly weak little photos from Chelyabinsk aren't worth a damn to anyone.
— Let him go photograph bears, — Marta said gloomily.
It was getting completely dark in the house, as if it weren't lunchtime but dusk.
— And I had a fight like that with Danylo once too, — Miyechka recalled.
— How?
— asked the grandmother.
— We were sitting by the river, and the sun was just setting.
A pang came over my soul, and I say: 'There's nowhere else like this.'
— Where were you sitting, over there by the old ravine?
— asked Lilichka.
— By it, — Miyechka confirmed and went on: — 'There's nowhere else like this,' I say.
And he says: 'But I don't care where I live.
I have no attachment to these places, I simply don't care: whether it's here by the ravine, or in Lisbon, or in Brno.'
— Even in Kostroma?
— I ask.
— So you, I see, are a damned cosmopolitan.
— 'I didn't say that.'
And I got up and left.
Well, so what?
I was young and headstrong, — Miyechka laughed.
— Just got up and left.
— And then what?
— Marta stood with her hand on her belly.
— He ran after me all the way to the village and kept saying that his grandfather was a Cossack.
— And that was the end of it?
— I still don't believe he had any Cossack in him.
— And I just taught Fumitaka to say the word 'thank you,' — said Marta.
And everyone fell silent.
Fumitaka
How Marta got together with Fumitaka was unclear.
He was quiet, friendly, handsome, with narrow shoulders, a long neck and fingers white as snow.
Marta was abrupt, with broad, thin shoulders, heavy hair and brows black as thunder.
But Fumitaka sat in an office, in India, where Marta sat too.
He went to yoga and meditated there for hours with his eyes closed.
Fumitaka avoided going outside.
Marta avoided going out there too.
So their relationship developed by the coffee machine.
— What do you mean, by the coffee machine?
— asked Lilichka.
— Once he came over, and I didn't walk away.
— God, why so blunt!
— Miyechka laughed.
— Better tell us how he banged twins into you, — said Lilichka.
— Just banged them in, that's how.
Damn.
— Do they have devils in Japan?
— asked the grandmother, who liked to compare different cultures.
— The devil knows, — replied Marta.
For the first few months Marta told Fumitaka nothing.
She refused to see him, said that was it, nothing would come of it, that she was unfit for relationships and had never sought them.
She didn't shout at him, but hissed these words at him through her teeth in such a way that it was impossible not to believe her.
But life in her relentlessly progressed, and there was nothing to be done about it.

Fumitaka looked at her sadly in the office, and then, having waylaid her after work, took both her hands and said he would go to Japan for two months to finish all his affairs there (the affairs turned out to be a dying father and his house), and would come back for her.

Being a measured person, he didn't even think that such a step on his part, when he resolved to cut all ties with his past life and sell the house where he'd grown up, might be taken as if he were running away.
When he returned to Delhi, Marta was no longer there.
And she'd already gone a week without answering any of his messages.
Fumitaka called Marta all the time.
At first she didn't pick up, and poor Fumitaka studied a map of Ukraine and tried to find the settlement of Rurynti.
That was exactly how, with his poor Japanese ear, Fumitaka had made out Marta's place of birth.
He had no chance whatsoever of finding this place on the map.
He went to a Russian lawyer in Japan, who promised, for eighty thousand yen, which at the time equaled about six hundred euros, to search Google Maps for Rurynti in Cyrillic.

When Fumitaka grew thoroughly downcast, counting out the money after the unsuccessful search, the Russian lawyer, whose name was Sergey, at first even thought of taking from the unfortunate Japanese man not eighty thousand but sixty, and then recalled the alliance with Hitler, realized that the Japanese man was looking for some Ukie woman, even brought up Pearl Harbor for some reason, and took from Fumitaka not eighty thousand but eighty-five.
When Fumitaka looked at him in surprise, Sergey explained briefly: 'For the electricity.'
And he so warmed himself by suppressing his own empathy that he added to himself: 'Fucking Jap.'
The fucking Jap bowed politely and stepped out again into life, to the futile search for the mysterious city of Rurynti.
When Marta finally picked up, Fumitaka spoke quickly, stumbled, and asked Marta to marry him, asking three times whether she'd given birth yet.

Marta, who that evening sat beside Lusya while she slept, was actively smothering in herself any longing for the kind, sensitive, handsome Fumitaka, but in the end sent him the coordinates of the place that Fumitaka called Rurynti.

Though she added that she hadn't given birth, and that she didn't need these children, and that she regretted that he'd ever been in her life at all, and that if he wanted, let him come and take these children to hell with him and feed them with fake breasts at his place in Japan.
— Rurynti, — said Miyechka, — so beautiful, as if it were somewhere in Italy!
In three days Greg was due to arrive.
And with him — Roberto and Marko.
Nati, Greg's mother, was pushing the idea that her son needed help.
— That elderly señora Teodora is over there!
— she shouted from early morning.
— She needs help.
— Mom, you never helped her.
— How dare you say such a thing about your own mother?
I bought her the best tomatoes at the market, — Nati flared up.
— I cooked pasta!
Greg understood that Nati had already decided everything.
And even if he didn't buy her a ticket himself, she would do it on her own and arrive the next day, saying that she couldn't leave poor elderly señora Teodora with two wild boys.
The wild one was only Marko, Lilichka's son with Ivan, while the fair-haired Roberto was calm, with half-closed eyelids.

And while Marta herself was trying to grasp the fact that Fumitaka was coming, and everyone around was talking about this incredible fact, Greg with the boys and the happy Nati, with her high teased blond hairdo and her eyes black as night, were already boarding a plane in Seville, while Lilichka drove out of the yard, setting a course for the airport.
Miyechka was cooking.
The grandmother sat at the table and counted: 'So, you, Lilya, Marta, Maria, Lusya — that's only five of us.'
— And you?
— said Miyechka, quickly washing potatoes for roasting.
— Oh, what about me?
— said the grandmother, as old people were wont to say, who liked to stress that 'don't brew coffee for me, because maybe I'll die before lunch.'
— Six of us, then, — Lilichka summed up.
— Well, Marta could be counted as two, or even three.
— As long as she sits on one chair, I won't do that, — Miyechka retorted.
— Maybe I won't even fit anymore, — said Marta from the next room, where she and Lusya were cracking nuts for a pie, and the shells flew under the bed.
— And guests, then, there are four, plus this one, oh, what's his name...
— Fumitaka, — Miyechka helped.
— Five.
— And altogether, then, there are eleven of us.
Lord, Solomiya, hurry up and finish washing that potato!
Maria was carrying chairs onto the terrace and setting up the table there.
It hadn't even managed to get dark before all eleven people were sitting at the table on the terrace.
Fumitaka, getting out of the car, bowed deeply to everyone.
And everyone bowed to him.
The grandmother bowed especially politely, because she very much wanted him to marry Marta.
Fumitaka also very much wanted to marry Marta.
Although it was impossible to make out what was inside: whether he really wanted it, or just wanted to atone for his sins before some Japanese gods of his.
Lilichka kept assuring Marta that she'd been married and had each time come back from it unharmed.
The grandmother said that as long as the children were born healthy she didn't care, though in reality this wasn't so.
Maria gave Marko and Roberto a dinosaur each in plaster, out of which they had to be chiseled with hammers.
Nati sat beside the grandmother and shouted into her ear.
As is typical of southern peoples, Nati thought that the Spanish words Teodora didn't understand could be compensated for by shouting.
So she shouted, gesticulating actively, and the grandmother sometimes winced at the loud sound.
Fumitaka was waiting for the moment to ask for Marta's hand.
He understood that that request over the phone didn't count.
He'd googled that in Ukraine you ask the parents for a hand, but so far he couldn't figure out which of all these people was Marta's mother, and there were no candidates for the father.
